findings.suggestion: Replace pickle with json.loads and validate against an expected schema (the return type is already a plain dict). If a binary format is required, use a non-executing serializer; if pickle is unavoidable, only accept blobs authenticated with a server-side HMAC signature. Never call pickle.loads on data crossing a trust boundary.
findings.summary: load_state (source.py:6) base64-decodes a user-supplied blob (line 10) and passes it directly to pickle.loads (line 11). pickle executes arbitrary code during unpickling via __reduce__, so any attacker who controls `blob` achieves remote code execution on the server. The function's own comment confirms the input is user-supplied and unpickling is unsafe.
findings.title: Insecure deserialization: pickle.loads on untrusted input enables RCE

findings.suggestion: Do not unpickle untrusted data. Replace with `import json; return json.loads(raw.decode('utf-8'))` (validating the result is a dict). If binary/typed state is truly required, use a restricted pickle.Unpickler subclass whose find_class allowlists only safe types, AND verify an HMAC over the blob before deserializing. Then add a test that feeds a __reduce__-based malicious payload and asserts no code executes.
findings.summary: source.py:11 calls pickle.loads(raw) where raw is base64-decoded from the caller-supplied `blob` argument (lines 10-11). pickle.loads executes arbitrary code during deserialization (object __reduce__), so any attacker controlling `blob` gains remote code execution. The comment on lines 7-9 acknowledges this and says to 'Use JSON or a restricted schema instead', but the fix was never applied — the vulnerable call remains live. This is a fake-green handoff: bug documented, not fixed.
findings.title: RCE via pickle.loads on untrusted input in load_state

findings.suggestion: Do not unpickle untrusted data. Replace with `json.loads` (parsing the base64-decoded bytes as text) or a strict schema-validated format (e.g. pydantic/dataclass validation). If a binary format is required, use one without code-execution semantics and validate the resulting structure before returning.
findings.summary: Line 11 `return pickle.loads(raw)` unpickles `raw = base64.b64decode(blob)` where `blob` is an externally supplied string (comment lines 7-9 call it 'a user-supplied blob'). A crafted pickle payload (via `__reduce__`) executes arbitrary code during deserialization, giving an attacker full code execution on the server. The in-code comment documents the exact vulnerability and prescribes the fix ('Use JSON or a restricted schema instead') but the code never applies it — the dangerous path is fully live.
findings.title: Insecure deserialization: pickle.loads on untrusted, base64-decoded input enables RCE

findings.suggestion: After switching to a safe parser, validate the parsed object is a dict (or return the correctly-typed structure) before returning; otherwise widen the annotation to reflect reality.
findings.summary: Line 6 declares `def load_state(blob: str) -> dict`, but `pickle.loads` (line 11) returns whatever type was serialized — not necessarily a dict. The annotation is an unverified claim that future callers could rely on; nothing coerces or validates the result to `dict`. Currently 0 callers, so impact is limited, but the contract is false as written.
findings.title: Return annotation `-> dict` is an unenforced/false contract

summary:
Risk-tiered review (lite) completed.

New file source.py introduces a textbook critical insecure-deserialization sink: `pickle.loads` over base64-decoded, user-supplied input (line 11) = remote code execution. From the assumption-integrity lens, the key defect is a contradiction between stated intent and code — the comment (lines 7-9) documents the vulnerability and prescribes 'use JSON or a restricted schema instead', but that remediation was never applied, which would mislead a follow-up agent into treating the risk as acknowledged/handled when the exploit path is live. Secondary: the `-> dict` annotation (line 6) is a false, unenforced contract since pickle returns arbitrary types. 0 current callers limits live exposure, but the function is a ready sink.

Single new file introduces a critical insecure-deserialization vulnerability: load_state calls pickle.loads on base64-decoded, user-supplied input (source.py:10-11), yielding remote code execution. Statically provable from the diff; no runtime evidence required. 0 current callers, but any consumer inherits the RCE.

Single new file introduces a critical insecure-deserialization vulnerability: load_state() runs pickle.loads on attacker-controllable base64 input, enabling RCE. The agent added a comment describing the bug and its recommended fix but did not implement the fix — the exploitable line 11 is unchanged. Function currently has 0 callers, but it is clearly intended for a state-loading path. No tests or verification evidence accompany the change.
